title: The Long-Chain Multiplication
concept: In BPTT, gradients flow backward through repeated multiplication of the recurrent weight matrix across time steps.

exercises.prompt: What is the name of the method used to calculate gradients in recurrent networks by going backward through sequence steps?
exercises.explain: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) is the algorithm used to train Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) by calculating gradients across sequence steps.

cards.title: Looking Back in Time
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how a robot remembers what happened ten steps ago? To learn from the past, AI has to look backward through time, creating a long chain of math that can easily break if we aren't careful!

cards.title: The BPTT Chain
cards.body: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) calculates how to update weights in a recurrent network. To see how past steps influenced the present, BPTT traces the signal backward, repeatedly multiplying the recurrent weight matrix at each time step.

cards.title: The Whisper Chain
cards.body: Imagine a whisper chain where 10 kids sit in a line. If each kid passes the message at only 90% volume (multiplying by 0.9 each time), the last kid hears almost nothing. In Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT), gradients are like that whisper, getting multiplied again and again by the same weight as they travel back in time.

cards.title: Tracing a 5-Word Sentence
cards.body: If a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) processes a sentence with 5 words, BPTT traces the gradient back through 5 steps. To update the initial step's weights, the gradient is multiplied by the recurrent weight matrix 5 times in a row! If that weight is small, the gradient disappears before it reaches the first word.

cards.title: Myth: Independent Steps
cards.body: Many believe that gradients in recurrent networks are calculated independently for each time step. In reality, because the same weight is reused, the math links all time steps together in a giant chain of multiplication. What happens at step 10 directly multiplies what happened at step 9, 8, and so on.

cards.title: Standard vs. Recurrent Flow
cards.body: Compare how weights are handled during backpropagation in a standard network versus a recurrent network over time.

left.text: Gradients pass through different weights at each layer once.

right.text: Gradients pass through the exact same weight matrix repeatedly across time.

exercises.prompt: How do gradients flow backward through time steps in BPTT?

options.text: By adding the weights together at the very end
By repeatedly multiplying the recurrent weight matrix across time steps
By ignoring the weights and only looking at the inputs
By squaring the weights at the final step only

exercises.explain: During BPTT, the gradient is multiplied by the recurrent weight matrix at every single time step we trace back, creating a long chain of multiplications.

exercises.prompt: True or False: BPTT requires multiplying by the same recurrent weight matrix multiple times to flow gradients back through a sequence.
exercises.explain: Because the same recurrent weight matrix is reused at each step, BPTT must multiply by this matrix repeatedly to calculate how past weights affected the present.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how gradients travel back through time in RNNs.
exercises.sentence: BPTT flows gradients backward through repeated weight matrix [blank] across time steps.
exercises.explain: BPTT propagates gradients backward by multiplying the recurrent weight matrix at each sequential time step, forming a long mathematical chain.

exercises.prompt: Which calculation represents how BPTT traces gradients back through multiple time steps?

options.text: A simple one-time multiplication of input and weights.
A chain of the same weight matrix multiplied over and over for each past step.
A random shuffling of all weights in the network.

exercises.explain: The first option represents a normal forward pass through a single layer, while the second option represents the long-chain backward multiplication characteristic of BPTT. The third is unrelated.

exercises.prompt: Why does the math of Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) lead to long-chain multiplications?

options.text: The recurrent weight matrix is multiplied repeatedly at each step backward in time.
It uses different weights at every step, preventing repeated multiplications.
It calculates gradients forwards instead of backwards.
It ignores older time steps entirely to speed up training.

exercises.explain: Because the gradient passes through the same recurrent weight matrix at every single time step, updating the network requires multiplying by this matrix repeatedly over the sequence length. This causes the gradient to either shrink to zero (vanishing) or blow up (exploding).
